Base: Builds are triggered manually or only on specific branches. Developers often build locally and deploy from their machines.
Beginner: CI exists but is fragile - builds break often and are not always investigated promptly. Some manual steps remain in the build process.
Intermediate: CI runs automatically on every push. Build failures are investigated within the same day. Build times are monitored.
Advanced: CI is fast (<10 min), reliable (>95% green), and runs on every PR. Flaky tests are tracked and fixed proactively.
Expert: CI is optimized with caching, parallelization, and incremental builds. Build health is a team-level KPI visible to everyone.

Base: No visibility into build health trends. Failures are noticed only when someone tries to deploy.
Beginner: Basic build status is visible (pass/fail) but no historical trends or dashboards.
Intermediate: Build success rate, duration, and failure reasons are tracked over time. Team reviews build health periodically.
Advanced: Build analytics are automated - trends, regressions, and slowdowns are surfaced proactively. Alerts on degradation.
Expert: Predictive build health - the team detects and fixes build degradation before it impacts developer flow.

Base: Code reviews are optional or inconsistent. Some changes go to production without any peer review.
Beginner: Code reviews happen for most changes but quality varies - some are rubber-stamps with no meaningful feedback.
Intermediate: All production changes require review. Reviews consistently check for correctness, readability, and test coverage.
Advanced: Reviews are timely (<24h), thorough, and constructive. Review quality metrics are tracked (comments, cycles, time-to-approve).
Expert: Code review is a learning culture - reviews spread knowledge across the team. Junior devs grow measurably through review feedback.

Base: Little or no automated testing. Manual testing before releases is the primary quality gate.
Beginner: Some unit tests exist but coverage is low and inconsistent across the codebase. No integration or E2E tests.
Intermediate: Unit tests cover critical paths. Integration tests exist for key workflows. Test coverage is measured and visible.
Advanced: Comprehensive test pyramid - unit, integration, and E2E tests with clear ownership. Coverage targets are set and met.
Expert: Test strategy is risk-based - coverage is prioritized by business impact. New features require tests before merge. Mutation testing or similar advanced techniques are used.

Base: Tests are often skipped because they are slow or flaky. Nobody trusts the test suite fully.
Beginner: Tests run but flaky tests are common. Developers re-run pipelines hoping for green. No systematic flaky test tracking.
Intermediate: Flaky tests are identified and fixed within a sprint. Test suite is generally trusted by the team.
Advanced: Test suite is highly reliable (>98% deterministic). Flaky tests are quarantined automatically and fixed with priority.
Expert: Zero-tolerance for flaky tests. Test infrastructure is treated as a first-class product with its own quality standards.

Base: No static analysis or security scanning in the development process.
Beginner: Static analysis tools exist but are not integrated into CI. Results are checked occasionally.
Intermediate: SAST and dependency scanning run automatically in CI. Critical findings block the merge.
Advanced: Security scanning covers SAST, dependencies, and secrets detection. Findings are triaged and resolved within SLA.
Expert: Security is shift-left - developers get inline feedback in IDE. Vulnerability trends are tracked. Regular security reviews complement automated scanning.

Base: Releases happen rarely (monthly or less). Each release is a significant event requiring coordination.
Beginner: Releases happen every 2-4 weeks, usually aligned with sprint boundaries. Some manual coordination required.
Intermediate: Releases happen weekly or more frequently. The process is mostly automated with minimal manual steps.
Advanced: Multiple releases per week are routine. Any commit that passes CI can be released. Release is a non-event.
Expert: Continuous deployment - every merged PR is automatically deployed to production. No batching, no manual gates.

Base: Deployments are risky - rollbacks require manual intervention and often cause downtime.
Beginner: Basic rollback exists but is rarely tested. Deployments occasionally cause incidents.
Intermediate: Rollback procedures are documented and tested. Canary or blue-green deployments are used for critical services.
Advanced: Zero-downtime deployments are standard. Feature flags decouple deploy from release. Rollback is automated and fast (<5 min).
Expert: Deployment safety is proactive - automated canary analysis, progressive rollouts, and automatic rollback on anomaly detection.

Base: High failure rate - hotfixes and emergency patches are frequent after releases. Root causes are rarely investigated.
Beginner: Hotfixes happen regularly but the team acknowledges the pattern. Some post-incident reviews occur.
Intermediate: Change failure rate is measured. Post-incident reviews happen consistently and result in concrete preventive actions.
Advanced: Low change failure rate (<10%). Most failures are caught in staging. Incident patterns are analyzed for systemic improvements.
Expert: Near-zero change failure rate. Proactive risk assessment before releases. Failure patterns from across the org inform preventive engineering.

Base: No awareness of code complexity. Hot spots accumulate unchecked. Developers avoid touching certain parts of the codebase.
Beginner: Some awareness of complex areas but no systematic tracking. Refactoring happens only when pain becomes unbearable.
Intermediate: Complexity hotspots are identified and tracked. The team allocates time for refactoring in sprint planning.
Advanced: Complexity metrics are monitored automatically. Refactoring is continuous - the team improves code health every sprint as part of normal work.
Expert: Architecture fitness functions enforce complexity boundaries. Technical debt is quantified and managed as a product backlog item with business trade-offs.

Base: Monolithic codebase with tight coupling. Changes in one area frequently break others. No clear module boundaries.
Beginner: Some separation exists but boundaries are inconsistent. Shared code creates unexpected dependencies.
Intermediate: Clear module/service boundaries are defined and mostly respected. API contracts exist between major components.
Advanced: Modules are independently deployable and testable. Breaking changes go through a deprecation process. Dependencies are explicit.
Expert: Architecture enables autonomous team delivery - each team owns a bounded context with well-defined interfaces. Cross-team changes are rare.

Base: Technical debt is not tracked or discussed. It accumulates silently until it causes incidents or blocks features.
Beginner: Technical debt is acknowledged in conversations but not systematically tracked. No dedicated time for debt reduction.
Intermediate: Tech debt items are captured in the backlog with clear descriptions. The team allocates a percentage of capacity to debt reduction.
Advanced: Tech debt is quantified (e.g., linked to complexity metrics, incident frequency). Debt reduction is balanced against feature delivery with stakeholder buy-in.
Expert: Tech debt is managed as a strategic investment - the team proactively prevents debt accumulation through architecture reviews, coding standards, and automated enforcement.

Base: Development environments differ significantly from production. 'Works on my machine' is a common phrase. Environment setup takes days.
Beginner: Development environments partially mirror production but key differences exist (different OS, database version, config). Setup takes hours.
Intermediate: Development environments closely match production in key aspects. Containerization or similar ensures consistency. Setup is documented and takes <1 hour.
Advanced: Environments are provisioned from code and are near-identical to production. New developer onboarding takes <30 minutes for environment setup.
Expert: All environments (dev, staging, prod) are provisioned from the same IaC definitions. Environment drift is automatically detected and corrected.

Base: Infrastructure is manually configured. Server setup involves following runbooks or tribal knowledge. Configuration changes are undocumented.
Beginner: Some configuration is scripted but not version-controlled. Key infrastructure components still require manual setup or configuration.
Intermediate: Core infrastructure is defined as code (Terraform, Ansible, etc.) and version-controlled. Changes go through review process.
Advanced: All infrastructure is code-defined, version-controlled, and deployed through CI/CD. No manual changes to production infrastructure.
Expert: Infrastructure code follows the same engineering practices as application code - tested, reviewed, and continuously deployed. Self-healing infrastructure.

Base: Minimal monitoring. The team learns about production issues from users or customer support.
Beginner: Basic monitoring exists (uptime, CPU, memory) but alerting is noisy or misconfigured. Alert fatigue is common.
Intermediate: Key business and technical metrics are monitored. Alerts are meaningful and actionable with clear runbooks. On-call rotation exists.
Advanced: Comprehensive observability - structured logging, metrics, and distributed tracing. Dashboards show real-time system health. Alert SLAs are defined and met.
Expert: Observability is proactive - anomaly detection, predictive alerting, and automated remediation. SLOs/SLIs drive engineering decisions. Observability data informs product decisions.

Base: No awareness of work-in-progress limits. Team members routinely juggle 3+ tasks. Context switching is the norm.
Beginner: The team acknowledges that too much WIP is a problem but does not enforce limits. Sprint boards show many items in progress.
Intermediate: WIP limits are defined and visible on the board. The team actively manages flow and pulls work rather than pushing it.
Advanced: WIP limits are respected and regularly tuned. The team uses flow metrics (cycle time, throughput) to optimize delivery pace.
Expert: Flow efficiency is a primary team metric. Bottlenecks are identified and resolved systematically. The team continuously experiments with flow improvements.

Base: No shared Definition of Done. 'Done' means different things to different team members. Incomplete work moves to the next sprint.
Beginner: A Definition of Done exists on paper but is not consistently applied. Items are marked done without meeting all criteria.
Intermediate: DoD is clear, shared, and applied to every Product Backlog Item. The team references it during Sprint Review.
Advanced: DoD evolves based on team learning. It includes non-functional requirements (tests, docs, security). The team holds each other accountable.
Expert: DoD is a living quality standard that automatically raises the bar. Automated checks enforce DoD criteria in the pipeline.

Base: No estimation or wildly inaccurate estimates. The team cannot predict when work will be done. Stakeholders are regularly surprised.
Beginner: The team estimates but accuracy is low. Sprints regularly have significant spillover (>30%). Scope creep is common.
Intermediate: Estimates are reasonable - sprint completion rate is >70%. The team tracks and learns from estimation misses. Scope changes are managed.
Advanced: High predictability - sprint completion >85%, epic delivery within 1 sprint of estimate. The team uses historical data to inform estimates.
Expert: Data-driven forecasting - the team uses throughput and cycle time distributions for probabilistic delivery dates. Stakeholders trust the forecasts.

Base: Knowledge is siloed in individuals. Key-person dependencies are high. Onboarding new team members takes weeks.
Beginner: Some documentation exists but is often outdated. Knowledge sharing happens informally and inconsistently.
Intermediate: Regular knowledge sharing practices exist (tech talks, pair programming, documented ADRs). Onboarding is structured and takes <2 weeks.
Advanced: Knowledge is systematically shared across teams. Architecture Decision Records, runbooks, and internal docs are maintained as part of daily work.
Expert: Learning organization - cross-team rotations, internal conferences, communities of practice. Knowledge sharing is part of the team's identity and rhythm.

Base: No clear ownership of services or components. When things break, it is unclear who should respond. Blame culture exists.
Beginner: Ownership is loosely defined. Some teams own services but on-call is not established. Incident response is ad-hoc.
Intermediate: Teams clearly own their services. On-call rotation exists with documented escalation paths. Blameless post-mortems are the norm.
Advanced: Strong ownership culture - teams are accountable for the full lifecycle of their services (build, run, monitor). Incident learnings are shared across org.
Expert: Teams proactively improve what they own - reliability, performance, developer experience. Ownership extends to the customer outcomes, not just the code.

Base: Improvement happens only in reaction to crises. Retrospective actions are rarely followed through. The team feels stuck.
Beginner: The team identifies improvements in retros but follow-through is inconsistent. Only the most painful issues get addressed.
Intermediate: Retro actions are tracked and completed. The team regularly experiments with process improvements. At least one improvement is shipped per sprint.
Advanced: Continuous improvement is embedded in daily work - the team improves processes, tools, and practices without waiting for retros. Improvement impact is measured.
Expert: The team is a learning machine - they run structured experiments, measure results, and share learnings across the organization. Innovation time is protected.

Base: Teams work in isolation. Cross-team dependencies cause delays and conflicts. Integration issues are discovered late.
Beginner: Some cross-team coordination exists but is reactive. Dependencies are managed through manual handoffs and meetings.
Intermediate: Cross-team dependencies are identified early in planning. Teams communicate proactively about shared concerns. Integration happens frequently.
Advanced: Teams are loosely coupled - cross-team dependencies are minimized by architecture. When collaboration is needed, it happens smoothly with shared practices.
Expert: The organization operates as a network of autonomous teams with aligned goals. Platform teams enable product teams. Cross-team friction is systematically reduced.
